data.title: Why do all RWA revenues ultimately go to Pendle?
data.description:
Author: Stacy Muur Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

The stablecoin market has reached $310 billion, but the vast majority of funds remain idle and yield zero returns. With the accelerated on-chain deployment of Real-World Assets (RWA), Pendle has become one of the core applications that has benefited the most. This article provides an in-depth analysis of the underlying logic.

The total market capitalization of stablecoins is as high as $310 billion, with most of the assets remaining idle and unable to generate any returns.

This situation is being rewritten: Real-world yield assets are officially being massively on-chain. US Treasury interest, private lending returns, monthly dividends from Nasdaq-listed companies… In the past, these assets were strictly monopolized by brokerage firms and institutional access requirements; now, a number of protocols are tokenizing them, allowing all DeFi users to equally access the same level of returns as traditional finance.

However, these assets have a fatal flaw: their yields are highly variable, not fixed. An annualized yield of 11% this month could drop to 9% next month. For institutional funds and project treasuries, fluctuating returns are unsuitable for financial planning and risk management budgeting. This is precisely the core market gap that Pendle fills. After various tokenized RWA assets are integrated into Pendle, users have two options: lock in fixed, stable returns and secure future cash flow; or bet on fluctuating interest rates and aim for potential upside. The same underlying asset allows for free choice of risk-return structure. The macro environment continues to empower Pendle: The scale of public blockchain tokenized real-world assets surpassed $23.6 billion in March, a 66% surge from January; JPMorgan Chase predicts that the interest-bearing stablecoin sector alone could absorb $1.5 trillion in incremental funds in the future; Bank of America's CEO warned that a massive outflow of $6.6 trillion in bank deposits may flood into the stablecoin market. Traditional banks already view on-chain interest-bearing assets as a real competitive threat to the industry. Top global financial giants have already deeply integrated into the Pendle ecosystem: Asset management giant Apollo, with its $785 billion credit fund, has integrated Pendle through Ember; Strategy, a US-listed company, uses Saturn and Apyx to tokenize its STRC dividends on Pendle; Paxos' compliant US Treasury stablecoin USDG was listed on Pendle, with its locked value exceeding $120 million in less than two months; Ethena's locked value through Pendle far surpasses all other DeFi protocols, peaking at $4.71 billion. The following outlines the core ecosystem layout currently in place and their respective core values.

1. Strategy's STRC dividends officially launched on Pendle and is tradable

STRC is a Nasdaq-listed preferred stock issued by MicroStrategy (now Strategy), with bond-like characteristics: users who purchase it receive monthly cash dividends with a fixed annualized return of 11.50%. Strategy will use all the funds raised from STRC to continuously increase its Bitcoin holdings, becoming a key bridge between the traditional equity market and the BTC hoarding track.

5. Two major US bills have been passed, and Pendle will become the core stronghold for stablecoin yields

First, look at the difference in actual returns, and the underlying logic of capital flow is clear at a glance:

  • JPMorgan Chase savings: 0.01% annualized

  • USDC wealth management on Coinbase and Kraken platforms at the end of 2025: annualized

  • USDC wealth management on Coinbase and Kraken platforms at the end of 2025: annualized

    • The Endgame

    According to risk level classification, Pendle has covered the complete return matrix:

    • Conservative and Stable: USDG US Treasury Fixed Income approximately 5.3%, Ethena sUSDe Total Annualized Return 4.25%

    • Medium and Balanced: Apyx Portfolio Dividends approximately 14.5%, Saturn STRC Dividends approximately 11%

    • High-yield aggressive strategy: Ember eTHIRD cross-basis strategy 20.7%, Apollo Private Credit eACRED annualized 5%–11%

    To date, Pendle's cumulative settlement returns have reached $69.8 billion. Grayscale, in its Q2 2026 watchlist, removed a large number of consumer-related projects but still included Pendle in its core financial sector holdings.

    US Treasury bonds, private credit, US stock market dividends, multi-asset quantitative strategies… four completely different underlying assets and differentiated risk structures ultimately converge on the same protocol. All institutions and funds share the same essential need: to transform fluctuating and uncertain real returns into plannable and budgetable fixed cash flows.

U.S. Treasury Secretary Bethrent has publicly stated that a total stablecoin market capitalization of $2 trillion is "perfectly reasonable"; BlackRock's BUIDL reserves are deeply integrated with the Ethena ecosystem; traditional financial giants are no longer waiting for regulatory approval and have already proactively migrated their funds on-chain. A massive amount of real-world yield capital is continuously flowing into the blockchain; and Pendle is the ultimate hub for the global shift from floating-rate to fixed-rate income.

data.title: DeepSeek is at it again as a "price killer," but this time it's not just about cutting prices.
data.description:
Author: Xiaojing

Tokens are reshaping the value coordinates of the AI ​​era. The release of DeepSeek V4 preview has once again made it a "price killer," but it has also brought new challenges to token pricing. The actual cost of the same number of tokens can differ by an order of magnitude in different systems, indicating that large-scale models are moving towards system-level pricing.

The release of DeepSeek V4 preview has once again driven down the price of large-scale models, which is very much in line with DeepSeek's "characteristics."

The release of DeepSeek V4 preview has once again driven down the price of large-scale models, which is very much in line with DeepSeek's "characteristics."

Image: Tokens are split into "New Calculation" and "Repeated Calculation"

If you only look at the price, V4 continues DeepSeek's consistent low-price strategy.

If you only look at the price, V4 continues DeepSeek's consistent low-price strategy.

In the domestic market, models like Alitongyi, Zhipu GLM, and Lunar Dark Side Kimi are priced roughly between 1-4 yuan for input and 4-12 yuan for output, while V4-Flash is priced at 1 yuan for input and 2 yuan for output, placing it at 1/3 to 1/4 of the industry average. The Pro version, priced at 12 yuan/24 yuan, is close to the flagship model, but million-level context is a default capability rather than a premium option. Globally, the comparison is even more pronounced, with prices roughly one-tenth to one-fiftieth of some competitors. For example, GPT-5.5's official website price is: $5 per million tokens for input, $0.5 per million tokens for cached input, and $30 per million tokens for output. Claude Opus 4.7 continues the pricing system of Opus 4.6, roughly $5 per million tokens for input and $25 per million tokens for output. While overseas flagship models are not entirely comparable in terms of capability ceiling, ecosystem maturity, and token utilization, price is not the only dimension. However, within the same set of Agent tasks, the difference in invocation costs directly impacts commercial viability. Overseas vendors also face pricing pressure: Sam Altman has publicly admitted that ChatGPT Pro subscriptions are unprofitable, and Dario Amodei has warned of "overly aggressive pricing" in the industry. To a certain extent, the system behind pricing includes computing power supply, R&D amortization, and market strategies. This is why the price advantage is even more significant this time. In 2024, the industry addressed "whether it can be used"; in today's Agentic AI paradigm, the more crucial question is "whether it can be scaled up." An agent task often involves dozens to hundreds of model calls, with a large amount of input coming from system prompts, tool schemas, and historical memory. This content is highly reusable, but it's precisely where the cost is most likely to "inflate." DeepSeek V4 focuses on compressing this cost of "repeated computation."

Image: DeepSeek V4 has turned "cost" into a variable that can be optimized by engineering. The left side shows capability alignment, and the right side shows the cost cliff. In a million contexts, inference computing power and cache usage have decreased significantly, making long-running tasks no longer grow at an exponential cost. This is also the real driving force behind this round of price wars.

Looking at the specific price evolution of its own products, this change can also be traced.

From the perspective of the specific price evolution of its own products, this change can also be traced.

The previous generation V3.2 had an input price of 2 yuan (cache miss) and 0.2 yuan (cache hit), with an output price of 3 yuan. V4-Flash, however, reduces the input price to 1 yuan and the output price to 2 yuan, the most direct change being a "half-down" of the input miss price. In multi-round agent scenarios, the cumulative input cost often accounts for the majority, making the leverage effect of this adjustment far greater than the apparent price reduction. The Pro version's pricing of 12 yuan for input and 24 yuan for output appears to be an order of magnitude more expensive than Flash, but DeepSeek wrote in its technical report that "the Pro version is constrained by high-end computing power capacity. It is expected that after the Ascend 950 supernode is mass-produced and deployed in the second half of the year, the price of Pro will be significantly reduced." This can be understood as the current price of Pro reflecting supply bottlenecks, not the true cost. The positioning of the two models is also very clear: Flash is designed for high-concurrency, low-latency batch tasks, while Pro is responsible for complex agent processes, long-chain code generation, and deep inference. According to technical reports, DeepSeek has begun evaluating the V4 code agent's capabilities using real-world R&D tasks and is directly benchmarking it against the Claude series in internal evaluations. Behind the "Price Killer" How did DeepSeek manage to drive down prices? Traditional attention mechanisms, when processing long texts, increase computationally with the square of the sequence length; for example, the computational cost of 1M tokens is 64 times that of 128K. This is why "million-context" architectures were difficult to commercialize in the past. The memory usage of the KV cache increases linearly with the sequence length; reaching 1MB requires either reducing concurrency or adding several times the number of machines, which is not cost-effective on paper. This is also why overseas vendors generally adopt a strategy of "short window by default, longer window with additional charges." Anthropic even directly sets up a separate tier for 200K and above, doubling the price. Image: DeepSeek V4's CSA (Compressed Sparse Attention) significantly reduces computational and caching overhead in long text scenarios by first compressing the KV cache and then using Top-k to select key contexts, calculating only the most important information. Simply put, V4's solution combines "compression" and "sparseness". First, compress the KV cache for every m tokens into a single compressed entry (CSA compression rate 4, HCA compression rate 128). Then, for each query, focus only on the top-k key entries for attention calculation. The former step reduces GPU memory usage, while the latter reduces computing power, thus overcoming two bottlenecks simultaneously.

Figure: DeepSeek V4's HCA (Recompressed Attention) compresses the KV cache limits of longer sequences into a small number of representations, further reducing computational and storage overhead while preserving local window information. It is a key path supporting the reduction of the cost of millions of contexts.

The technical report shows that in a 1M context, V4-Pro's single-token inference FLOPs are only 27% of V3.2's, and its KV cache usage is only 10%; V4-Flash is even more aggressive, with FLOPs at 10% of V3.2 and KV cache at 7%. Combined with infrastructure layer optimizations such as FP4 quantization-aware training, the Muon optimizer, and the self-developed mega-kernel MegaMoE, V4 optimizes and compresses costs across the entire training-to-inference chain. The lower price is a natural consequence of the architecture's cost. A core member of a major domestic modeling company told Tencent Technology, "The pricing of APIs for major domestic models (including their own) mainly depends on cost control. No company is willing to 'buy prices' regardless of cost. Therefore, cost advantages achieved from the underlying technology are extremely important." Zhou Jingren, CTO of Alibaba Cloud Intelligence, also emphasized, "Every price reduction is a very serious process, requiring consideration of the overall industry development, feedback from developers and enterprise users, etc. It's not a price war." Why is this "price reduction" even more important? From the demand side, systematically lowering prices is more urgent at present. Deloitte's latest Token Economics report cites AT&T as an example: after introducing the Agent system, the company's daily token consumption increased from 8 billion to 27 billion. An analysis by Stevens Institute of Technology points out that agent systems suffer from a "quadratic token growth" trap in multi-turn dialogues: by the 10th round, the amount of tokens used in a single call can reach seven times that of the 1st round. The model price determines whether an agent can be commercially viable. A report in CIO magazine three weeks ago quoted Ayesha Khanna, CEO of AI solutions company Addo AI, as saying, "If you run a persistent agent that interfaces with cutting-edge model APIs, high token consumption, long contexts, multi-step inference, and heavy output will rapidly deteriorate the economics. In some cases, the cost per task can be more expensive than having a person do it." This is currently the most realistic bottleneck for agent commercialization: the technology may work, but the financials don't make sense. Looking back at V4's moves, almost all of them target this industry bottleneck: making millions of contexts a default capability eliminates the need for agents to pay a premium for long contexts; the cache hit input price is reduced to the lowest level in the industry, matching the characteristic of agents repeatedly using the same system prompts in their scenarios. The technical report also specifically mentions that V4 fully retains all reasoning content in tool call scenarios (V3.2 discards it at the beginning of each new user message), which is also to adapt to the multi-round call requirements of agents. Can V4 lower the overall cost line of Agentic AI? Ultimately, there is another important question: can V4 lower the overall cost line of Agentic AI? This time, the situation may be much more complex. First, we need to see if other vendors follow suit. If this round of price reductions for V4 triggers similar synchronized price cuts, the overall cost curve of the industry will truly shift downwards. However, as analyzed above, this time the model price is more determined by the cost structure, and model manufacturers have limited room for short-term profit margin compression, with limited room for follow-up. Secondly, there is the supply of high-end computing power. As DeepSeek stated in its technical report, the current service throughput of V4-Pro is limited. Whether the low price of Pro can be stably supplied depends on the progress of the mass deployment of domestic computing power such as the Ascend 950 supernode in the second half of the year, as well as DeepSeek's engineering progress across hardware platforms. Section 3.1 of the technical report clearly states that DeepSeek has verified fine-grained expert parallel solutions on both NVIDIA GPU and Huawei Ascend NPU platforms. This is also the first time DeepSeek has included Ascend and NVIDIA in its hardware verification list, attempting to decouple the inference path from a single hardware dependency. If this proves effective, its long-term value to the domestic large-scale model industry will be even greater. Thirdly, can the token structure in Agent scenarios be further optimized? Current Agents consume a lot of tokens, with a significant portion of this waste stemming from the Agent architecture itself. Beyond model price reductions, how the Agent uses tokens is another matter entirely. Even if V4 drives the unit price to rock bottom, poor Agent design could still lead to uncontrolled billing. This is the significance of the currently popular Harness system. Deepseek V4 preview has indeed lowered prices on the price list, making millions of contexts a default capability, and achieving output prices below one dollar/millions of tokens, all while having an architectural foundation and not relying on subsidies. However, this time, lowering costs across the entire industry is not so simple; it faces a more complex systemic challenge.

data.title: Hayes: War Injects Liquidity, BTC Year-End Price Target $125,000
data.description:
Compiled by: Jinse Finance

Arthur Hayes, founder of BitMEX and chief investment officer of Maelstrom, delivered a speech at the 2026 Bitcoin Conference in Las Vegas. He stated that, with wartime defense spending and the relaxation of new regulations in the US banking industry injecting new liquidity into the financial markets, he expects the price of Bitcoin to reach $125,000 by the end of the year.

In his speech, Arthur Hayes shared his views on Bitcoin and the market, taking into account recent factors such as the Iraq War, the development of artificial intelligence, changes in Federal Reserve policy, and adjustments in banking regulation, clearly expressing a bullish stance on Bitcoin. Arthur Hayes focused on analyzing the impact of liquidity changes on Bitcoin: Before the war, the deflationary effect of artificial intelligence (impacting the SaaS industry and triggering credit deflation) caused Bitcoin to fall by about 50% from its all-time high in October last year, while the Nasdaq index remained flat; after the outbreak of war, wartime inflation became the dominant factor, and Bitcoin outperformed the Nasdaq and SaaS stocks. Arthur Hayes also interpreted the stance of incoming Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin Warsh, believing that he is not the hawkish figure the market fears, but rather neutral. The Fed will reduce its balance sheet through the replacement of reserves, Treasury bonds, and repurchase agreements, and, constrained by the Treasury Department, this will not affect debt sales, resulting in a neutral net impact on liquidity. Meanwhile, the US deficit will reach $1.9 trillion in fiscal year 2026, and the Trump administration has significantly increased the defense budget to $1.5 trillion, with wartime spending driving up demand for commercial bank loans. He stated that the "Enhanced Supplemental Leverage" regulations, which took effect on April 1st, allow banks to increase leverage. S&P Global estimates this will generate $1.3 trillion in new loans, and the bank loan multiplier effect (approximately 3 times) could create $4 trillion in liquidity, far exceeding the credit losses caused by AI deflation. Finally, Arthur Hayes believes Bitcoin will break out of its consolidation phase, with a year-end price target of approximately $125,000, and remains optimistic about its overall trend. The following is the full text of his speech, compiled by Jinse Finance (AI-assisted). Good morning, everyone. I hope you're all still somewhat sober after your weekend in Las Vegas. I just finished the ski season and have this short break before heading back to the beach for another three months, and I thought, okay, I need to refresh my perspective on the market. So, I recently watched a new zombie movie, *28 Days Later*. That's basically the inspiration for my speech title. You'll see what I mean shortly. Basically, I've spent several days thinking about how I think money printing will evolve, taking into account the situation in the field of artificial intelligence, the Iran war, and various contingencies, and that's what this talk is about. Obviously, I've become slightly bullish, and I'll explain why in the next 15 minutes. Clearly, we can't ignore the ongoing war. So, before I get to my final point, I have to make a few assumptions in this talk. First, I hope we don't all die in a nuclear catastrophe. If we really did, there would be no investment opportunities, so it doesn't really matter. So, let's put that particular concern aside for now. Second, the market will treat this event as a short-term thing, whatever that means. Now it's time to think about money creation and printing, and what that means for Bitcoin. Every morning when I wake up, if I want to dissect what's actually happening, at least for my own portfolio, regarding the war, I just pull up this chart I created on my Bloomberg terminal: the six-month WTI crude oil futures contract minus the near-month contract. I'm very interested in this spread and how it changes. I only care about whether enough goods and oil are getting through the Strait, regardless of what propaganda I read from Donald Trump or Iran about the actual situation there. I only look at prices, and as we've seen, things are improving, meaning near-term prices are converging towards far-term prices, which indicates that yes, things are bad, but not terribly bad. So I can ignore it and move on to other things. Every time I'm in office, the talk is about printing money. Compared to when I wrote my last article about four weeks ago, my thinking has evolved to the point that, **in the medium term, liquidity will turn positive**. So, **on the negative side, there is AI deflation**. People keep talking about all those knowledge workers who have already lost or will lose their jobs because of the efficient and cheap models that enable knowledge work. I wrote an article a few months ago about my expectations for the potential damage from these job losses. I'll put some charts in a bit, but that's on the negative side. I think that could be a problem worth hundreds of billions of dollars for the banking system. I'll elaborate on the **Federal Reserve** later. There's a lot of unease in the market about incoming Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin Warsh and whether he's a hawk or a dove. I'll put his comments in the right context; basically, he's neutral, which is neither good nor bad for liquidity. Those who are going crazy because Warsh is a super hawk haven't really grasped the underlying logic. Then there's **commercial bank lending**. Why will it increase? Why will the war economy in the US and abroad drive banks to lend more to those involved in various arms production and the raw materials needed to manufacture those weapons? Finally, there are **changes in bank regulation**, which will allow banks to increase leverage on their balance sheets. So, here's a chart I've been looking at since last October. The red line is the Nasdaq index, the gold line is the Bitcoin price, and the white line is the US technology SaaS stock ETF. Now, most people, at least institutional investors, believe Bitcoin is highly correlated with the Nasdaq, and it has indeed performed that way over the past four or five years. But since Bitcoin hit its all-time high of $126,000 last October, it has fallen by about 50%, while the Nasdaq has remained flat. So, broad-based large-cap tech stocks are holding up. But if you look at the types of tech stocks that have been hit hard, they are all SaaS companies, the companies that produce products that AI can now accomplish for $10 a month, whereas they used to charge $10,000 or some outrageous amount. These stocks have been battered. I think this points to a credit deflation event that central banks haven't recognized, and they haven't printed enough money, which has driven Bitcoin down. And this was before the war. I've cut this chart to February 28th. Here's another wish of mine: I want to fire all my human accountants and lawyers. I've spent too much money on this stuff. I can't wait for AI to take over. This will have a very bad impact on anyone lending money to people who earn very, very high salaries. This is basically my thought process regarding AI as the new subprime mortgage and what that might mean for the commercial banking system. I think this macro narrative dragged down Bitcoin from October until late February, before the start of the Iraq War. But since the war began, Bitcoin has outperformed other assets. It outperformed the red Nasdaq and SaaS stocks. Basically, I think Bitcoin is now focused on wartime inflation. What's changing now? Now that the US and many other countries have explicitly acknowledged they are at war, their defense spending is insufficient, and they need to print more money to make more bombs, that's where AI is put aside. Next is the Federal Reserve. When Kevin Warsh was nominated, I think it was in January of this year, everyone started to go crazy because during his tenure as a Federal Reserve governor—I think from the 2008 financial crisis until this president—he had been critical of the Fed's very large balance sheet. He has publicly stated that he believes the Federal Reserve's balance sheet is too large and that he must find a way to shrink it, while simultaneously lowering interest rates. Now, if you've read my articles, you know I'm a staunch advocate that the quantity of money is more important than its price. Therefore, I'm more concerned with his comments on the balance sheet than the direction of short-term interest rates. Thus, if the market believes that Warsh's actions at the Fed will reduce the dollar liquidity circulating in the system, then they will be bearish on Bitcoin and other risk assets. This is the kind of discussion we've seen in the media about the hawkish Fed that will emerge after Warsh takes office in May. Now, I don't think so. I believe that, essentially, the Fed will replace reserves, Treasury bonds, and repurchase agreements with these and put them into the commercial banking system, and they will do this with the help of new regulations concerning how banks hold assets on their balance sheets and how much capital they need to hold for those assets. Finally, I think the most important thing to understand about what Warsh would or wouldn't do at the Fed is that he has a very substantial hard constraint: he needs to work with Scott Bessant of the Treasury to ensure that any actions he takes on the Fed's balance sheet don't impair Bessant's ability to sell billions, even trillions, of dollars in bonds. So, it's a very simple balance sheet. There are no numbers above because I know it's a bit complicated for some people. On the assets side, there are Treasury bonds, mortgage-backed securities, and repurchase agreements. These things help people finance the purchase of Treasury bonds. On the liabilities side, there are bank reserves, the Treasury's general account (the government's checking account), and currency in circulation. Basically, since 2008, the Fed has increased its liabilities (i.e., bank reserves) and purchased assets from the banking system. These assets are Treasury bonds, mortgage-backed securities, and repurchase agreements. When Warsh says the balance sheet is too big, he means the Fed holds too many of these debt securities and he wants to be able to shrink the balance sheet. Again, he could do this by selling bonds, but that would severely disrupt the markets. Or, I think he might be implying that he will be swapping with the US banking system. Currently, commercial banks' balance sheets also have Federal Reserve reserves as assets. Banks have approximately $3 trillion in reserves on their books with the Federal Reserve. On the funding side, they have loans, deposits, and shareholder equity. For a given balance sheet size, you have to have a certain amount of equity to match it. That's the capital adequacy ratio. So, what needs to happen is a swap between the Federal Reserve and the banks. Banks need to get rid of reserves, reduce their need for reserves, and replace those reserves with Treasury bonds and repurchase agreements. And this is precisely what's being driven by deregulating the US commercial banking system. So, whenever you hear Bessant or any other monetary official in the US government talk about deregulation, they mean that we want to allow the banking system to absorb all this debt we're creating and remove it from the Federal Reserve's balance sheet. The final state is that US commercial banks take the baton of money creation from the Federal Reserve, their balance sheets holding Treasury bonds and repurchase agreements, and their liabilities being deposits and shareholder equity. The point of all this is that the net impact on dollar liquidity is neutral. Nothing is sold, nothing is bought. It's just a substitution. Purely a fictitious operation at the regulatory level about who is allowed to hold what. But at the end of the day, Warsh could stand up and tell people he designed a smaller Fed balance sheet. But for us investors, in reality, we only care about what the net effect is? The net effect is nothing. Furthermore, Warsh won't clash with Bessant. They've had this picture with Jerome Powell's face before. We just need to replace Powell's face with Warsh's. Ultimately, we've issued $38 trillion in debt, and you need to fund the government. The Fed will do what's required: ensure the markets are orderly so people can buy that debt. Now look at spending. This is a chart of the fiscal year, from October to September of the following year. As we've seen, from the COVID-19 pandemic to this presidency and now, the deficit is the largest in U.S. peacetime history, and the deficit in fiscal year 2026 will be slightly higher than in fiscal year 2025. The point of all this is that the U.S. Treasury is not reducing spending. Donald Trump isn't talking about how he will drastically cut spending. That so-called "Department of Government Efficiency" thing is over from last year. It's all forgotten. Now it's all about wartime spending. His new defense budget is about 50% higher than the previous one, reaching $1.5 trillion. This doesn't sound like the Treasury and politicians are working together to reduce spending so the Federal Reserve can shrink its balance sheet. So all the talk about the Fed shrinking its balance sheet is meaningless, because politicians and the Treasury they fund are constantly increasing the total amount of debt in the market.

Another chart. Who is buying debt? Foreigners aren't buying as much debt as they used to. I've excluded some countries that are typically used for hedge fund basis trading.

Another chart. Who is buying debt? Foreigners aren't buying as much debt as they used to. I've excluded some countries that are typically used for hedge fund basis trading.

... You can see that the proportion of foreign-held debt remains roughly the same at 25%, while the total debt has increased significantly. This essentially means that all this debt needs a new, price-insensitive buyer: the U.S. commercial banking system. The banking system is now able to increase its debt because of a new regulation that just took effect on April 1st, called "Enhanced Supplemental Leverage" (ESLR). This essentially allows banks to hold fewer reserves and other types of assets to offset loans and other items on their balance sheets. This means that large banks like JPMorgan Chase and Citigroup can absorb more Treasury bonds and repurchase agreements in the market and take the baton from the Federal Reserve. For smaller banks, the lending engines of the U.S. economy, they can increase the size of their construction and industrial loans. S&P Global estimates that this ESLR balance sheet reduction will generate $1.3 trillion in new loans. So, why would banks have loan demand? One of the criticisms of this analysis from some of my other macroeconomic friends is that they claim the banking system isn't creating enough loans, or that there isn't enough demand. Well, we have a huge source of demand: the U.S. War Department. They not only put equity in certain deals, but they also guarantee the purchase of output. Banks see a company with a secured client—a government that can print money—and they lend to them. They also lend to miners who extract critical resources used to make bombs. Finally, all AI capital expenditures now become national security concerns. So when a hyperscale computing company can't finance its debt with free cash flow and goes to the market for financing, they find large banks with huge balance sheets willing to back it. So, look at construction and industrial loans; I think you can get that data from the Federal Reserve every week. This is just a chart from Bloomberg. Credit must flow. The advantage of bank lending is that it has a higher multiplier than central bank lending, empirically about 3 times. We've already seen that empirically. This means creating about $4 trillion, which is more than the amount of credit that AI might destroy by taking people's jobs, which is why I'm becoming more bullish on Bitcoin. I have a few seconds left. To reiterate, my liquidity chart bottomed out around November of last year, coinciding with Bitcoin's. I believe we've experienced some volatility. We've gone through a battle. Now it's time for a breakout. That's why I believe Bitcoin will go higher. I think my year-end price target is around $125,000 or something like that. Anyway, it doesn't really matter anymore. Thank you all very much.

data.title: OpenAI’s Sam Altman Apologizes for Failing to Alert Police Before Deadly Tumbler Ridge Shooting-But Is ChatGPT Really To Blame For This Tragedy?
data.description:
OpenAI CEO Sam Altman has issued a public apology following revelations that the company failed to alert authorities about a ChatGPT account linked to the suspect in the February mass shooting in Tumbler Ridge, a tragedy that left eight people dead.

Sam Altman's apology letter to the Canadian town of Tumbler Ridge after OpenAI did not report trans school shooter Jesse Van Rootselaar's violent chats with ChatGPT. pic.twitter.com/n2CVe2QRyN

— Libby Emmons (@libbyemmons) April 25, 2026

In a letter released Friday, Altman admitted OpenAI should have notified law enforcement after banning the account of 18-year-old Jesse Van Rootselaar in June 2025 for activity tied to the “furtherance of violent activities.”

Months later, the suspect allegedly carried out a deadly attack, first killing her 39-year-old mother and 11-year-old stepbrother, at their home, before going to the nearby Tumbler Ridge Secondary School and opening fire.

Five children and one educator were killed at the school before Van Rootselaar died by suicide. Twenty-five others were injured. Altman acknowledged the company's failure to report the account leading to the tragedy.

"I want to express my deepest condolences to the entire community. No one should ever have to endure a tragedy like this. I cannot imagine anything worse in this world than losing a child."

BREAKING: Mass shooting at British Columbia high school leaves 10 dead, including suspect

Police say six victims were found inside Tumbler Ridge Secondary School, with others discovered at a linked home; the gunman died from a self-inflicted injury as dozens more were treated… pic.twitter.com/2jSFpd3fKj

— Catch Up (@CatchUpFeed) February 11, 2026

After flagging the account, the company considered notifying the Royal Canadian Mounted police, but ultimately determined the activity did not meet its threshold for a “credible or imminent threat.”

The decision not to notify authorities is now at the center of growing criticism, as questions mount over whether earlier intervention could have altered the outcome.

Altman said he has since spoken with local leaders, including Mayor Darryl Krakowka and British Columbia Premier David Eby, who conveyed the scale of grief and anger within the community. While expressing condolences, Altman also pledged to work more closely with governments to prevent similar incidents—though concrete policy changes remain unspecified.

AI Safety Under Intensifying Scrutiny

The incident is rapidly becoming a flashpoint in the broader debate over AI accountability. As generative AI systems scale globally, regulators and lawmakers are increasingly questioning where responsibility lies when warning signs emerge inside private platforms.

AI Impact Summit 2026 | “What we do with Artificial Intelligence in the present matters more than what it can do in the future,” says Prime Minister Narendra Modi, drawing a parallel with nuclear power while stressing responsibility in AI development.#NarendraModi… pic.twitter.com/6JqRnXFRUE

— CNBC-TV18 (@CNBCTV18News) February 19, 2026

Recent legal and regulatory developments underscore the urgency. Investigations have been launched into whether AI systems encourages violent actors, while lawsuits against companies like Google over its Gemini chatbot and scrutiny of OpenAI’s own systems highlight mounting legal exposure across the sector.

A lawsuit filed by the family of Jonathan Gavalas alleges Google's AI encouraged harmful behavior that posed a risk to public safety and ultimately led to his suicide. https://t.co/QrwsDPW8Y2

— CNET (@CNET) March 5, 2026

At the same time, academic research has warned that certain AI models may reinforce paranoia and dangerous beliefs, raising concerns about how effectively current safeguards handle real-world risk scenarios.

Premier David Eby described Altman’s apology as “necessary, but insufficient,” reflecting a growing sentiment that voluntary commitments from AI firms may no longer be enough.

OpenAI CEO Sam Altman issued an apology letter to the people of Tumbler Ridge. The apology is necessary, and yet grossly insufficient for the devastation done to the families of Tumbler Ridge. 1/2 https://t.co/bqHoqQ5j08

— David Eby (@Dave_Eby) April 24, 2026

As OpenAI faces increasing regulatory pressure—and with Altman preparing for a high-profile legal battle with Elon Musk—the case is likely to shape how AI companies define their duty of care in the years ahead.

data.title: CFTC Chair: Innovation Working Group Engages in Policy, Encourages Communication Within the Crypto Community
data.description:
Compiled by: Jinse Finance

Mike Selig, Chairman of the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), stated at the 2026 Bitcoin Conference in Las Vegas on Monday that the CFTC has "turned a new page" in digital asset regulation, emphasizing the necessity for coordination between the CFTC and the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC).

Selig pointed out that for markets trading products that combine characteristics of both commodities and securities, the two agencies need a harmonized framework, rather than each developing overlapping or conflicting rules.

In a dialogue with the moderator, Selig outlined the regulatory measures and philosophies implemented since taking office 100 days ago, with the core objective of ending the past "enforcement-style regulation," promoting the standardized development of the crypto industry in the United States, and consolidating the U.S.'s global leadership position.

In terms of cross-agency collaboration, Mike Selig and SEC Chairman Paul Atkins jointly relaunched the "Project Crypto" initiative, signing a memorandum of understanding to coordinate regulatory approaches between the CFTC and SEC, reduce market friction, ensure consistency in rules in areas such as decentralized finance and the use of crypto asset collateral, avoid uncertainty for market participants caused by regulatory conflicts, and prevent a recurrence of situations where regulatory agencies are "weaponized" against the industry. Regarding the classification of crypto assets, the two parties issued joint guidelines, clearly distinguishing between securities and non-securities crypto assets. This resolves the past controversy surrounding the SEC's generalization of most crypto assets as securities, provides compliance clarity for market participants, reduces compliance costs for companies, and clarifies the regulatory boundaries for the listing and sale of new crypto assets. Once assets are no longer subject to the issuer's commitments, they can be traded as commodities, improving industry liquidity. Mike Selig emphasized protecting self-custody rights and the interests of software developers, issuing relevant guidelines and "no-action letters" to provide a safe harbor for self-custody wallet software developers, allowing them to provide services without registration, supporting crypto assets as collateral in the derivatives market, enabling flexible transfer of self-custody assets between exchanges, and practicing the core principle of blockchain: "trusting code, not intermediaries." At the legislative level, Mike Selig believes that market structure legislation (such as the Clarity Act) is crucial, as it can solidify current favorable regulatory policies into law, resist future policy reversals by governments, and centralize the regulation of crypto intermediaries from the decentralized licensing system of 54 states to the federal CFTC, avoiding regulatory conflicts among states and providing a stable environment for industry development. He called on Congress to pass relevant legislation to clarify the legal status of crypto assets and consolidate the United States' position as the "crypto capital of the world." Finally, Mike Selig stated that the CFTC has established an Innovation Working Group, inviting industry participants and private sector leaders to participate in policy-making and encouraging the Bitcoin community to proactively communicate and report business frictions. The CFTC will actively provide support to help the industry take root and develop in the United States. The following is the full transcript of the dialogue, compiled by Jinse Finance (AI-assisted). I. Welcome—A New Era for the CFTC Host: Good morning, everyone. It is an honor to have with us Mike Selig, the 16th Chairman of the Commodity Futures Trading Commission. Mr. Chairman, you have just published a commentary on your 100th day in office. For Bitcoin enthusiasts who have faced regulatory uncertainty and sometimes even hostility for over a decade, tell us what the past 100+ days have meant to you? Mike Selig: Well, we're at a pivotal moment. We've seen the impact of the previous administration on the cryptocurrency industry, and now the U.S. is the world's cryptocurrency capital. We've ended the era of "regulation through enforcement." Gary Gensler is no longer at the SEC. Our government has leaders who support cryptocurrency and innovation. You just heard Cash Patel and Todd Branch speak. We're turning a new page. Clear rules for this new asset class are crucial for it to thrive in the U.S. That's what we're here for. So, in my first 100 days as chairman, I made sure to work with SEC Chairman Paul Atkins to develop clear classification standards for crypto assets, defining what constitutes a security and what doesn't. We established a new system for introducing brokers. We allow digital wallet providers to offer their software in the U.S. without having to register with us as introducing brokers. We're just getting started. We have a lot more work to do. And the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) has a special role to play with Bitcoin itself.

II. How Bitcoin Became a Commodity in 2014

Host: Back when other regulatory agencies either ignored or were sometimes hostile to the industry, the CFTC began studying Bitcoin in 2014, 2015, and 2016, saying: You know, this is a commodity. It should be treated like all other commodities within our framework. Has this history influenced your current approach? After all, you worked at the CFTC and are now its chairman.

Mike Selig: Absolutely. Many years ago, I had the opportunity to work with Chris Giancarlo, who was a commissioner and later became chairman. We were both very fascinated by Bitcoin. I read the Bitcoin white paper around 2011 and have been deeply involved ever since. In 2013 and 2014, I had the opportunity to work at the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), where we saw that Bitcoin truly didn't look like a security. It's a decentralized asset, something entirely new, and it looks a lot like other commodities in our markets—gold, silver, oil. Unlike any entity that controls the price, its price is based on supply and demand. So, years later, the CFTC became the first agency to approve a federal regulatory framework for crypto assets, allowing Bitcoin futures contracts to be traded on exchanges we regulate. I truly believe the CFTC was a pioneer in the cryptocurrency space. We are very fortunate that Congress has legislation pending, which we hope will pass, expanding our powers and ensuring that this asset class in the United States is resilient to future changes through clear rules and a regulatory framework.

III. Project Crypto and the SEC-CFTC Memorandum of Understanding

Host: You mentioned earlier your collaboration with Chairman Atkins. Before you took over the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, you served as his chief legal counsel and also as the chief legal counsel for the SEC's Crypto Working Group. While there, you helped launch Project Crypto and issued many important guidelines, including proof-of-work mining. In January of this year, after joining the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, you launched Project Crypto with the SEC and signed a memorandum of understanding to facilitate cross-agency coordination across the industry. This is very exciting for lawyers and policymakers. But for market participants and yourself, why is this coordination so important for the future of Bitcoin, cryptocurrencies, and other regulated markets, and even for how we build good government and good policy? Mike Selig: We saw the SEC weaponize its enforcement apparatus under the previous administration, really trying to push cryptocurrencies overseas. Then we saw banking regulators deprive people working in the industry of access to banking services, making it virtually impossible for them to get a bank account. That was “Operation Chokehold 2.0.” Of course, we ended that under this administration. But we need to ensure our agencies are not weaponized against the industry again in the future. That’s why Chairman Atkins and I came together to create this new initiative called “Project Crypto.” It was originally an initiative launched by Chairman Atkins at the SEC, and I was able to help when I was there. Now our two agencies are joining forces to help strengthen the domestic crypto asset industry and ensure that things like the previous administration’s war on crypto never happen again. But it’s equally important that we have some legislation to help solidify that and solidify our leadership on crypto issues. The real purpose of “Project Crypto” and the memorandum of understanding between our agencies is to ensure that the SEC and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission don’t do completely different things when dealing with crypto issues. We need to harmonize our approach. We need to ensure that, for example, our approach is similar in areas such as decentralized finance, blockchain-based trading, and the use of stablecoins and Bitcoin as collateral. I've only been in office for a little over 100 days, and we've already harmonized our approach, for example, in terms of capital requirements being similar when using different stablecoins and crypto assets as collateral and margin. So we're really working together to ensure that whether you're trading in securities or commodities, you don't have to comply with different types of truly inconsistent rules and regulations. Host: This is a subtle point that's easy for someone unfamiliar with legislation to overlook. Legislation has a specific purpose. The Commodity Futures Trading Commission's governing law is the Commodity Exchange Act, and the Securities and Exchange Commission's is the Federal Securities Act. We have about 20 different banking laws, and then there are tax laws. So, under different laws, Bitcoin might be treated as a different thing. Therefore, this harmonization makes things easier for market participants, for users, intermediaries, and everyone else. Mike Selig: And if your chairs can cooperate, it makes things much easier. Host: Exactly. Mike Selig: We want to reduce friction in the markets. We don't want to face inconsistent standards because you're trading in a securities environment or a commodities environment. Sometimes that distinction makes sense, but where we can harmonize and adopt alternative compliance practices across institutions, it's better for market participants and ultimately for the American people. Host: Yes. You mentioned the classification guidelines you released earlier. Why is this important? And it's joint guidance, which is fantastic. What do you think this unlocks for the wider industry? Mike Selig: Yes, the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) stood up a long time ago, as we've said, and declared Bitcoin a commodity. But unfortunately, that's not the case for many other assets. We've seen many new crypto assets, like Ethereum, Solana, Zcash, and so on. The SEC has historically considered many of these to be securities. Of course, this has been challenged in court, and many of these cases actually stem from SEC lawsuits against exchanges like Coinbase and Gemini. We're now joining forces to ensure the two agencies are aligned on what constitutes a security and what doesn't. This creates more clarity for all market participants. Many businesses are hesitant to establish operations in the U.S. because they issue tokens, and if those tokens are classified as securities, they have to register, provide disclosures, and compliance costs become very high. So, clarity is crucial for the certainty of business and knowing your obligations. Of course, commodity laws weren't designed for crypto assets; they were designed for assets like oil, gas, gold, and silver, which aren't based on centralized issuers or companies. Many crypto assets, while some are certainly affiliated with companies, mostly operate in decentralized environments where you trust the code and the network's operation. Therefore, providing this clarity is crucial. We also provide clarity regarding the listing and sale of new crypto assets. Sometimes the sale itself is subject to securities laws because you are selling certain promises and guarantees along with the asset. But once the asset begins trading on the market and these promises are no longer attached to it, the asset itself can be traded on the commodity market without the disclosure obligations under securities laws. This is very important for providing liquidity to the asset class and certainty for the American people.

V. Protecting Self-Custodial Rights and Software Developers

Host: You recently provided guidance for software developers. You mentioned the "no-action letter" from introducing brokers in your opening remarks. This is fundamentally about protecting developers. I know this is a particular concern for you, but how do we create an environment in the U.S. that ensures software developers can build here with confidence while maintaining this environment that protects self-custodial and non-custodial services? Mike Selig: Yes, **self-custody and software development in the United States must be protected.** Our nation is built on the idea of ​​private property. We must prevent governments or anyone else from seizing someone's crypto assets, depriving them of banking services, or cutting off their access to the financial system. Therefore, creating a safe harbor and clarity for software developers and providers of various types of self-custody software applications must be a priority for us as regulators in the United States. So, one of the first things the committee did after I took office was to issue a new position of action on software services in the self-custody wallet space. Typically, to access our derivatives markets, you go through a broker, who connects to an exchange, and it's all done in custody. You put in capital, you put in margin, and you trade in the derivatives market in custody. We've never had the ability to do it in a self-custody way, where you hold your own assets and transfer those assets to an exchange based on your trading activity, with margin moving between your wallet and the exchange. Now we have this new system: If you are a software developer providing a self-custodial wallet product, as long as the software development company meets certain criteria, the product and the software developer do not have to register with the commission. This will allow the use of Bitcoin and other crypto assets as collateral in our derivatives markets. You will be able to self-custody these assets, unlike some of our current markets where you use custodial wallet providers tied to exchanges. Here, you can use your own self-custodial wallet. We believe this will really help demonstrate the use cases for cryptocurrencies and blockchain because the core idea has always been that you can hold your own assets and rely on on-chain technology—trusting the code rather than your intermediaries.

VI. Why Market Structure Legislation Matters Now

Host: This is the regulatory level. And in Washington, all eyes are on the Clarity Act at the legislative level. I think this is a very complex topic. But one thing people should note is that it establishes strong regulation of intermediaries while also protecting the right of individuals to self-custody their assets. It protects software developers, and in practice, it should be able to foster industry growth. The Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) plays a very important role in this. Could you talk about how you see the Clearness Act's role in unlocking the CFTC's influence on Bitcoin and other crypto markets? Mike Selig: Well, as I said at the beginning, I do believe that the U.S. is the world's cryptocurrency capital today thanks to President Trump's leadership, but we must maintain that position. It's crucial to enact legislation to protect software developers, to protect this asset class, and to set clear rules. Because if we don't seize this opportunity to build some real legal safeguards, much of what we, as regulators, do could be overturned by the next administration. You'll see someone like Gary Gensler go back to the SEC or the CFTC, and he can overturn everything we've done, which is a real threat to this asset class. Of course, this asset class can operate outside of government agencies; it's designed to operate based on code, etc. But the reality is, governments have enormous power; they can really weaponize law enforcement and regulatory agencies to target people in this industry. We cannot allow this to happen again. I believe the best way to protect this asset class from future changes in the United States is to enact legislation that clearly declares crypto assets to be legitimate technology that can be used by ordinary Americans in business and finance. This would truly lay the foundation for the flourishing of this asset class in the United States. VII. Federal Clarity vs. 54 State Licensing Systems Host: The Clarity Act elevates the regulation of intermediaries from the state level—currently there are 54 different licensing jurisdictions (if you want to operate throughout the United States and its territories)—and concentrates most of the power in the Commodity Futures Trading Commission. I think this is a very welcome outcome for market participants. Mike Selig: Exactly. If you think about the historical trajectory of crypto assets, this is absolutely crucial. It all started with state systems. You have New York's BitLicense, and you have all those states' money transfer laws applied to cryptocurrency exchanges. Therefore, if they want to launch an exchange or custodial wallet, and even some non-custodial software providers have encountered trouble with various states, they have to cater to 50 different states and figure out their obligations. And now we're seeing that as the federal government adopts a more favorable system for cryptocurrencies, states are turning around and suing many exchanges. We just saw New York file lawsuits against two of the largest cryptocurrency exchanges in the US. They're essentially continuing the legal battle where Gary Gensler and the SEC stopped. So, establishing a federal system that protects you, that you have a federal license, and that states have no power to regulate these exchanges and assets after the fact, will be very important for the operation of the asset class in the United States. Mike Selig: Yes, it's very important to engage. We want to understand what you're building, what you're doing, the frictions you're experiencing in your business, and how we can help. In the past, as I said, you came in and got a subpoena, and the government tried to use the full force of the law against you. That's not what we're trying to do here. I've formed an Innovation Task Force, and we have leaders from industry, from the private sector, who go inside the government to look at these issues from a policy perspective. We've also drawn some of the best and brightest people within our agencies to help solve these problems. So we're inviting you to meet with the Task Force, tell us what you're doing, and let us find a way to help your business establish itself in the United States long-term. Moderator: Mr. Chairman, thank you for your public service and principled leadership. It's an honor to sit with you today and to see the progress you're making in Washington. Mike Selig: Yes, thank you. It's nice to be here.

data.title: ZetaChain Pauses Mainnet Cross-Chain Transactions After Attack On GatewayEVM Contract Sparks Fresh Bridge Security Fears
data.description:
Cross-chain activity on ZetaChain came to a sudden halt after a security incident hit its GatewayEVM contract, forcing the network to pause transfers while teams worked to contain the issue and assess potential exposure.

Cross-Chain Pause After Contract Attack Raised Alarm

ZetaChain confirmed that an attack targeted its GatewayEVM contract, prompting an immediate suspension of cross-chain transactions on its mainnet.

The team said the issue affected internal wallets only, not user deposits or funds.

In a public update on X, it stated:

“There was an attack against the ZetaChain GatewayEVM contract today that impacted the internal ZetaChain team wallets only. We’ve already blocked the attack vector so no more funds can be compromised and will be releasing a detailed post mortem after we have completed our investigation.”

As a precaution, the network paused cross-chain activity while engineers moved to patch the vulnerability and review the system.

There was an attack against the ZetaChain GatewayEVM contract today that impacted the internal ZetaChain team wallets only. We've already blocked the attack vector so no more funds can be compromised and will be releasing a detailed post mortem after we have completed our…

— ZetaChain 🟩 (@ZetaChain) April 27, 2026

Internal Wallets Hit But Were Users Exposed?

ZetaChain maintained that no user funds were impacted during the incident, despite early social media warnings suggesting a broader exploit.

The project added:

“As a precaution cross-chain transactions are currently paused on ZetaChain. Investigation is still ongoing and at this time no user funds were impacted by this attack.”

However, uncertainty spread quickly after a warning circulated on crypto channels claiming the bridge had been exploited, triggering calls for users to move assets.

The lack of an immediate full breakdown added to market concern, a common pattern in bridge-related incidents where information often lags behind speculation.

What Is The GatewayEVM Contract?

The compromised component, GatewayEVM, acts as the entry point for cross-chain activity between ZetaChain and external EVM-compatible networks such as Ethereum and Polygon.

It works alongside other core components including ERC20Custody, which holds tokens bridged into the network, and ZetaConnector, which manages token flows tied to ZETA across connected chains.

In simple terms, it is one of the main pathways used when assets or messages move between blockchains, making it a high-value target in interoperability networks.

🚨. @ZetaChain has been exploited. Based on initial analysis, the following outlines the root cause.

Root Cause
The core vulnerability lies in the call function of ZetaChain's GatewayZEVM contract, which lacks both access control and input validation. This allows any arbitrary… https://t.co/U63DKIfgDZpic.twitter.com/WbAHdiciRc

— SlowMist (@SlowMist_Team) April 28, 2026

Network Status Shows Systems Running But Transfers Frozen

Despite the incident, ZetaChain’s infrastructure remained largely operational.

Its status page showed all 11 mainnet components and testnet systems running at full uptime.

Only cross-chain transactions were disabled, with the incident listed as “identified” and “ongoing” for several hours while engineers confirmed the attack vector and prepared a fix.

At the time of reporting, the pause had lasted around nine hours since detection, with the team indicating a patch was being prepared.

How Much Was Lost In The Attack?

While ZetaChain has not confirmed exact figures, blockchain data cited by DefiLlama estimated losses of around $300,000.

NEW: @ZetaChain has reportedly been exploited, with at least $300K drained so far. pic.twitter.com/KcAaB1iOqD

— SolanaFloor (@SolanaFloor) April 27, 2026

The project has not publicly validated this figure and has said a detailed post-mortem will follow once the investigation concludes.

ZETA, the network’s token, holds a market capitalisation of roughly $73.3 million, with about 1.36 billion tokens in circulation out of a total supply of 2.1 billion.

Why Bridge Security Is Under Pressure Again

The incident lands during a turbulent period for decentralised finance, with April 2026 already recording one of the highest levels of crypto-related exploits in recent years.

Earlier this month, the Kelp DAO cross-chain exploit drained about $292 million, an incident later linked by multiple cybersecurity firms to the Lazarus Group.

Kelp DAO lost about $294M in a @LayerZero_Core bridge exploit.$ZRO dropped from $2 to $1.4, and a whale holding a $ZRO long on #HyperLiquid got partially liquidated, losing $2.88M.

The whale is still holding the position with a floating loss of over $750K. Overall, the whale… https://t.co/psD7U02563pic.twitter.com/1k7O4LJprw

— Onchain Lens (@OnchainLens) April 19, 2026

That attack alone triggered wider stress across DeFi, including heavy exposure risks on lending platforms such as Aave.

In response, an industry coalition named “DeFi United” began raising funds to cover losses, with contributions from major ecosystem players and founders helping push recovery efforts past $160 million.

defiunited.eth is now open for contributions. All contributions are going towards DeFi United relief efforts to restore rsETH and safe DeFi.https://t.co/gdkGe5pi0ehttps://t.co/lOlsI3SmVH

— Stani (@StaniKulechov) April 24, 2026

Across the month, more than $606 million has been lost across 12 separate incidents, according to DefiLlama data, with cross-chain bridges continuing to represent one of the most targeted attack surfaces in the sector.

Cross-Chain Design Remains A Persistent Weak Point

This latest ZetaChain incident adds to a long list of bridge and interoperability exploits in crypto history, including Ronin, Wormhole, and Nomad, each of which resulted in large-scale losses.

The repeated pattern has kept pressure on developers to strengthen verification systems, improve audit standards, and introduce safer withdrawal and transfer mechanisms for cross-chain liquidity.

ZetaChain has said it will publish a full post-mortem once its investigation is complete, while cross-chain services remain paused pending final confirmation of system integrity.

data.title: SEC Chairman: Embrace Digital Asset Innovation, End Enforcement-Driven Regulation
data.description:
Compiled by: Jinse Finance

At the 2026 Bitcoin Conference in Las Vegas on Monday, U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) Chairman Paul Atkins stated that the SEC is committed to embracing digital asset innovation, ending its enforcement-oriented regulatory model, and working with the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) to bring a clear regulatory framework to the U.S. cryptocurrency market.

In a conversation with Perianne Boring, founder and CEO of Digital Chamber, Paul Atkins described the SEC's previous attitude towards digital assets as a failure.

He stated that the SEC will abandon its past "ostrich policy" and "enforcement-style regulation" model, and instead embrace digital asset innovation, committed to strengthening cooperation with the CFTC and clarifying regulatory boundaries to retain relevant U.S. companies. He also explained the core of the security definition (focusing on project commitments rather than the token itself), mentioned the advancement of token classification guidelines, on-chain experiments, and related exemptions, emphasized the importance of market structure legislation for policy stability, and looked ahead to the development of blockchain instant settlement and tokenized stocks, with the core goal of promoting the U.S.'s global leadership in the digital asset field. The following is the full transcript of the dialogue, compiled by Jinse Finance (AI-assisted). Host: Chairman Atkins, thank you very much for coming to Las Vegas to speak with us. Paul Atkins: It's my pleasure. It's great to see you all.

Host: I think this is your first time—and I believe it's the first time the Chairman of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) has spoken at a Bitcoin conference. At least that's what people say. Yes, we are honored to have you here.

Paul Atkins: Yes. Anyway, thank you very much.

Host: The relationship between the Bitcoin community and the SEC has always been quite interesting. You succeeded—what should we call him? Gary Gensler. He wasn't very open or accepting of digital assets. He certainly did his best to slow down the development of this innovation. You joined the Commission as part of the Trump administration. This was our first president to support Bitcoin and cryptocurrency, and you were really at the forefront of implementing this policy at the SEC. Before we dive into the details, could you briefly talk about your guiding principles as Chairman of the SEC and your views on digital assets? Paul Atkins: Okay. I think I should put it this way: This is a new day for the SEC. I think if you look at the last ten years or so, at the beginning, the SEC was like an ostrich burying its head in the sand, thinking maybe it would all just disappear on its own. Then came the phase of regulation through enforcement, and I think the SEC saying, “Oh, come talk to us,” was a bit insincere. You know, we have a simple form on our website called S-1. But that’s not an easy form to fill out. It takes a lot of lawyers and accountants to complete. It’s actually designed for IPOs, which is great for that kind of scenario, but it’s not very suitable for the digital asset space, especially tokens. So, we’re taking a new approach. We embrace innovation and genuinely want to help related businesses stay in the United States. We’re responding to the president’s challenge—he wants digital assets to be done in the United States, using American technology. So we’re really working towards that goal. You just saw Michael Selig from the Commodity Futures Trading Commission; he’s a great guy. He was in my office before, and I was very pleased that the President appointed him as Chairman of the Commodity Futures Trading Commission. He's done an excellent job, and we're working closely together. So, believe it or not, in Washington, the Commodity Futures Trading Commission and the Securities and Exchange Commission have never really collaborated historically. I'd rather compare it to two fortresses with no man's land in between, and the crossfire between the fortresses destroyed all those new products that could have been developed 40 years ago. So, we're going to change that now. We're going to really collaborate, set new benchmarks for the nation, and truly embrace change. Host: Yes, you've put it very well, the collaboration with the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, and how important that is for regulatory clarity, innovation, market structure, and America's leadership in this industry. In my view, there has indeed been competition between the Securities and Exchange Commission and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission in the past, a struggle for jurisdiction. One agency would say it's our jurisdiction, the other would say it's ours, and then the private sector would wonder, who is the regulator? Who should we contact? This created enormous uncertainty and risk for businesses, leading many to either close down or leave the market, and those fortunate enough not to be pursued by law enforcement were left with no other option. That was the strategy at the time. Paul Atkins: Absolutely right. So the real problem is that the definition of a security is a very technical issue, and there isn't much guidance available. Therefore, the real breakthrough we're trying to push for largely stems from an old case from 1946 called *SEC v. Howe*. Mr. Howe had an orange grove in Florida, and many people wanted to participate and own a small stake. Basically, securities regulations mention various things like stocks, bonds, etc., but they introduce a concept called an "investment contract" without bothering to define it. So this case was essentially the U.S. Supreme Court trying to define it. Therefore, when we set out to apply this to digital assets, we found that in Mr. Howe's case, the investment contract wasn't the oranges themselves—here equivalent to tokens, Bitcoin, or anything else—but rather Mr. Howe's commitment to his investors and the entire ecosystem that investors expected. So we've now applied this to the entire digital asset space, and through our partnership with the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, we released a joint statement in which we explained what tokenized securities are. So that was a major breakthrough. We released that statement earlier this year, and now we're moving on to other exciting things, like truly allowing businesses to experiment on-chain, build their tokenized securities, and trade them on-chain within the U.S. We plan to release an exemption for innovation in the coming weeks. Additionally, it will allow financing through our on-chain token sales. These things are in the works and will be rolling out soon. But we know that just a few minutes ago, a regulation was discussed here, the Clarity Act, which is currently under consideration in Congress. We really need Congress to regulate this space. We are ready, willing, and able to interpret their regulations and translate them into rules that people can rely on and pursue their innovative ideas. Again, it's within the U.S., they don't have to go overseas. That's the core idea that's really important here.

Host: Yes, there's a lot to elaborate on. So this isn't just about providing regulatory clarity and helping companies understand when the SEC's jurisdiction begins and ends, and where the Commodity Futures Trading Commission's jurisdiction lies. This has always been one of the most complex and challenging issues for everyone. But we need to take more than just steps toward regulatory clarity; we need to be able to bring more financial applications on-chain. There's a lot to cover. Perhaps we could start with token classification guidance to help clarify which agency has jurisdiction over different types of tokens—digital securities, digital commodities, digital collectibles, and so on. Regarding digital commodities, in your token classification guidance—you actually announced this a few weeks ago at my Washington Blockchain Summit—thank you. That was a very exciting announcement, and something we've been talking about for a long time: establishing a classification system. The SEC has clearly listed some tokens that are considered digital commodities. We've seen the market react to this, and now these tokens are trading at a premium, especially in Asian markets. So, this raises another question: what if the token I'm interested in isn't on that list, but we believe it meets all other criteria and therefore is still a digital commodity? What are your comments on how market participants can gain more clarity and how they should interpret this list? Paul Atkins: Yes. This is a very important question. So, in developing this explanatory document, we gave many examples, which are just illustrative. This doesn't mean it's a fixed list. But we talked about the principles behind it, tracing back to the Howey Test, which isn't about the orange itself, but about the commitments surrounding it. So, what's really important is that things can start with those commitments, within that framework, but then those commitments can be fulfilled, or they can disappear and become unenforceable. That's the core work we've done about the nature of securities, and that's the approach we've taken. So what we're trying to do is exclude these digital goods, digital instruments, digital collectibles, and of course, stablecoins. These are clearly not securities. Stablecoins, because Congress has passed and the President has signed the Genius Act, is a big step forward. For the first time, the U.S. has recognized an entire class of digital assets and provided a framework for things like being able to trade them. So this is very important. Therefore, we focus on tokenized securities. We're trying to define them as not belonging to the other four categories. That's the key. It's a principle-based approach. So, if people have questions, we welcome them to seek clarification, but we hope that this document we've released will make things clearer. Obviously, things can always be improved and further explained.

Host: Okay. Let's talk about the Market Structures Act. Senator Cynthia Lummis was just here giving us a timeline of the bill's passage, and she expects progress in May, a Senate vote in June, and hopefully it will be on the president's desk shortly after passing the House. I mean, a lot of things have to happen. I used to be a congressional staffer, and I understand the legislative path; a lot of things have to be aligned to get it done. Of course, we hope this will happen, but it's not a done deal. So, in case the Market Structures Act doesn't pass, I know a lot of people are worried, especially those who have been through several cycles: What will happen after the Trump administration? What if the new administration is as hostile to cryptocurrencies as the Biden administration, and we have a new SEC chairman like Gary Gensler who wants to shut it down? That's why a lot of people think the Market Structures Act is so important for making all the work of this administration resilient to future changes. So, could you talk about this concern? What are you doing to mitigate this in case the Market Structures Act doesn't pass? How much of a reaction should people reasonably expect if the bill doesn't pass? Paul Atkins: Good question. I think you all realize that elections do have consequences, and potentially huge ones. What I mean here is, who could have imagined that 10 years ago, the U.S. government—including us, the SEC, banking regulators, and Congress—would have made such a complete turn, almost a 180-degree turn? So, that's wonderful. The SEC has a lot of room to maneuver under the regulations, we can be flexible, but we are bound by existing powers that, while revised over the years, are essentially still the framework of the 1930s. So, that's why having a regulation that protects future developments from adverse effects, and then we can utilize the new powers, and the flexibility that the bill provides. We can work with the SEC to harmonize and clarify definitions, and build upon that. But to reiterate, nothing provides a better guarantee for the future than codified law, coupled with sound judicial advice, and the mechanisms of the entire court system that inscribe the provisions of codified law in stone. So all of this is extremely important, but we are focused on streamlining processes, improving efficiency, and helping innovators innovate so they can do it confidently, rather than being killed by those who jealously cling to existing ways of doing things. However, we want to ensure we are at the forefront of innovation in the United States. Host: Speaking of being at the forefront of innovation, one area in our market that is very well positioned to benefit from the power of blockchain is tokenized stock, which you mentioned earlier. The committee is in a very important position to facilitate this innovation. One challenge I see is that tokenized stock involves many stakeholders, many participants, and many steps between transaction execution and settlement, with intermediaries taking fees along the way. Could you talk about all the stakeholders in this process? Can we achieve the full benefits of blockchain (i.e., instant settlement) while also managing all these different participants who either have to participate or whose business models may be altered or eliminated as a result? Paul Atkins: Yes. That's a great question. I know we'll probably be closing up soon. Host: Yes, time flies. Paul Atkins: For me, blockchain—distributed ledger technology—is, in my opinion, the most exciting aspect of all this. I'm a little hesitant to say this at a Bitcoin conference, but you know, I'm basically agnostic about specific tokens themselves. Let the market decide what's best. But being able to achieve what we call T+0, instant settlement for many instruments—maybe not for everything—but instant delivery to payment, receipt to payment, I think is the most exciting because it reduces risk in our financial system, because every second between transaction time and clearing settlement is a risk borne by investors and both parties. In short, we're working hard to promote and achieve this within the United States. There are many existing players, including current exchanges, etc., but we also want to open it up to them. This is also a way for them to reduce their own market risk. In addition, there are many good ideas from other diverse groups, from the native crypto and native blockchain communities. We hope that all these different flowers can bloom, which will solidify the United States' position as a market for ideas and financial innovation, which is better for the world, for consumers, for investors, and for our overall economy. I think that's what we're trying to do.
